skill.name: auth-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze authentication and authorization designs for common security weaknesses and practical remediation steps.
skill.summary: Reviews auth flows, access controls, sessions, tokens, and password handling for security gaps.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and cryptography patterns. Review shows they are Markdown examples inside a defensive authentication audit guide. No prompt injection, executable helper script, credential exfiltration endpoint, or malicious runtime behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Exploit Examples in Skill Text
medium_findings.description: The skill includes forged JWT, IDOR, and brute-force request examples. They are presented as defensive audit material, but they could guide offensive testing if used outside an authorized environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit attack demonstrations, but surrounding text frames them as report evidence and remediation guidance. No evidence found that the skill executes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static command findings point to fenced JavaScript, shell, and usage examples. They are not standalone scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows documentation and fenced examples rather than executable skill files. The only scanned file is SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Illustrative
low_findings.description: The process.env references show recommended secret handling for sessions, JWTs, OAuth, and password reset flows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each process.env reference appears inside an auth security example or remediation snippet. No evidence found of outbound transmission of real environment values.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Placeholder or Local Examples
low_findings.description: The URLs and fetch calls use provider.com, example.com, or relative API paths. They are educational examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are embedded in educational snippets and report examples. No evidence found of a hidden webhook or unauthorized exfiltration destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Negative Examples
low_findings.description: The MD5, SHA-256-without-salt, and deprecated cipher snippets are explicitly labeled as insecure patterns to remediate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings and labels mark these snippets as bad examples. The skill recommends bcrypt, Argon2id, secure token generation, and stronger secret handling.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Finding Is Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM database alert points to a report heading, not filesystem access or registry access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 454 is inside a Markdown report template. No evidence found of code that reads Windows SAM or other local credential stores.

low_findings.title: Wildcard Permission Checks Misclassified as Cryptography
low_findings.description: The wildcard matches are RBAC and ABAC permission examples, not weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged wildcard values are authorization rule checks. No cryptographic primitive is used at these locations.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: No evidence found of override commands, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. The opening instruction only defines the intended security-review role for the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unverified JWT Payload Parsing Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates parsing a JWT payload with Buffer.from before explaining that signature verification is required. This is a dangerous pattern when copied without the remediation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code pattern is real and security-sensitive, but the text explicitly labels it as bad or vulnerable. The risk is copy-paste misuse, not hidden execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Brute Force Shell Loop Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a shell loop that posts many login attempts with curl. It is an attack example used to explain missing rate limiting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop is explicit and executable if copied, but it appears under an Attack heading in a defensive report template. No evidence found of automatic execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Secret Handling Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows session, JWT, OAuth, and reset-token secret patterns using environment variables. This is appropriate guidance but still touches sensitive configuration concepts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code references secret-bearing environment variables. The context is remediation guidance, so the finding is a caution rather than a confirmed data-access issue.

content.value_statement: Authentication and authorization bugs can expose accounts, sessions, and private data. This skill helps review auth flows and produce focused fixes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews password hashing, password reset, and brute-force protection patterns.
Checks session configuration, cookie flags, and session fixation risks.
Analyzes JWT, OAuth, API key, and token validation patterns.
Evaluates RBAC, ABAC, ownership checks, and privilege escalation risks.
Maps findings to OWASP, NIST, PCI-DSS, CWE, and remediation priorities.
Produces structured security reports with severity, impact, and fixes.
limitations: It reviews provided code or designs and does not run a full penetration test.
It cannot prove runtime behavior without logs, configuration, and deployed context.
It may need framework details to judge middleware order and session storage.
It includes dual-use examples for authorized security work only.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Auth Review
use_cases.description: Review login, session, password reset, and authorization code before a production release.

use_cases.title: Access Control Design Check
use_cases.description: Validate RBAC, ABAC, ownership checks, and admin routes during feature development.

use_cases.title: Compliance Gap Assessment
use_cases.description: Compare authentication controls against OWASP, NIST, and PCI-DSS expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Review Login Basics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a login flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this login flow for password storage, account enumeration, rate limiting, and session handling issues.

prompt_templates.title: Check Token Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these token authentication files for signing, verification, expiration, audience, issuer, refresh token, and secret handling risks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Authorization Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these routes and middleware for missing authorization, IDOR, unsafe role changes, ownership bypass, and privilege escalation paths.

prompt_templates.title: Create Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized authentication and authorization remediation plan with severity, business impact, affected files, and test recommendations.

output_examples.input: Review a password reset implementation with predictable tokens and no rate limiting.
output_examples.output: The report identifies weak token generation, account enumeration, missing throttling, and session invalidation gaps. It recommends random tokens, hashed storage, generic responses, short expiry, and reset attempt limits.

output_examples.input: Analyze API routes that rely on frontend role checks.
output_examples.output: The report flags missing server-side authorization and privilege escalation risk. It recommends middleware enforcement, ownership checks, denied role updates, and role-based tests.

output_examples.input: Assess JWT middleware that decodes tokens before trust decisions.
output_examples.output: The report explains why unsigned payloads cannot be trusted. It recommends signature verification, allowed algorithms, issuer checks, audience checks, and short expiry.

best_practices: Provide the relevant auth code, middleware order, configuration, and threat model together.
Use findings only for authorized systems and include business impact with each issue.
Ask for remediation tests after fixes, especially for roles, ownership, and token expiry.
anti_patterns: Do not use the attack examples against systems you do not own or have permission to test.
Do not rely on frontend role checks as proof of authorization enforcement.
Do not treat generated findings as final proof without code review and runtime validation.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews authentication, sessions, tokens, password handling, access control, and related compliance concerns.

faq.question: Can it replace a penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It supports code and design review, but it does not replace authorized runtime testing.

faq.question: Does it run security tools automatically?
faq.answer: No evidence found of built-in scripts. The skill is a Markdown guide for AI-assisted review.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: It contains dual-use exploit examples, although they are framed as defensive training and remediation material.

faq.question: What inputs improve results?
faq.answer: Provide auth routes, middleware, session configuration, token code, role rules, and expected user permissions.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
